title: Transfers of energy and matter
summary: An ecosystem is an open system: energy FLOWS through it in one direction while matter CYCLES round and round. This lesson builds the whole C4.2 picture — trophic levels and the producers, consumers and decomposers that occupy them; food chains and food webs; why only about a tenth of the energy at one trophic level ever reaches the next, and how that ceiling limits food-chain length and shapes pyramids of energy; the recycling of nutrients through the carbon cycle; and, briefly, how human activity disturbs these flows. The worked examples and the model answer are laid out exactly the way our marking engine awards Paper 2 marks, so you can see how each distinct point earns credit.
learningObjectives: Explain why an ecosystem is an open system through which energy flows but matter cycles.
Identify trophic levels — producers, primary/secondary/tertiary consumers and decomposers — and use food chains and food webs to represent feeding relationships.
Explain why only about 10% of energy passes to the next trophic level, and how this limits food-chain length and shapes pyramids of energy.
Describe the recycling of nutrients through the carbon cycle and the central role of decomposers.
Outline briefly how human activity disturbs energy flow and the carbon cycle.
Present explanations as distinct, creditable points the way our marking engine awards Paper 2 marks.
levelNote: Common to SL and HL at this level.

simpleExplanation.title: Energy flows, matter cycles
simpleExplanation.summary: Sunlight enters an ecosystem, is captured by producers, and is passed along a food chain to consumers — but almost all of it leaks out as heat at every step, so it never comes back. The atoms those organisms are built from behave differently: carbon, nitrogen and the rest are used again and again, released by decomposers and taken up once more by producers.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Picture a relay race run with a leaky bucket of water. The water is the energy: each runner (trophic level) pours what is left into the next runner's bucket, but so much sloshes out on the way that after only a few handovers there is barely a splash left — which is why the race has few legs. The runners' numbered vests are the matter: they are collected at the end, washed, and handed back out for the next race. The water is spent and gone; the vests go round again.
steps: Producers capture light energy and convert it to chemical energy stored in their biomass (photosynthesis).
At each feeding step only about 10% of the energy is passed on; the rest is lost — mostly as heat from respiration, some never eaten, some never digested.
Because so little energy remains, food chains are short and a pyramid of energy is always upright.
Decomposers break down dead matter and waste, releasing the nutrient atoms so producers can take them up again — matter cycles while energy does not.

flashcards.back: A system that exchanges both energy AND matter with its surroundings. Ecosystems are open: energy enters as light and leaves as heat, and matter can enter and leave — but crucially, energy FLOWS through while matter CYCLES within.

The feeding position an organism occupies in a food chain — e.g. producer, primary consumer, secondary consumer, tertiary consumer. It describes how many steps energy is from its original source (usually the Sun).

An autotroph that makes its own organic compounds from inorganic ones, usually by photosynthesis (e.g. plants, algae). Producers form the first trophic level and are the entry point of energy into most ecosystems.
Heterotrophs that obtain energy by feeding. A primary consumer eats producers (herbivore); a secondary consumer eats primary consumers; a tertiary consumer eats secondary consumers.

An organism (chiefly saprotrophic bacteria and fungi) that feeds on dead organic matter and waste, breaking it down and releasing inorganic nutrients back to the environment. Decomposers are the engine of nutrient cycling.

A food chain shows a single linear sequence of feeding, with arrows pointing in the direction ENERGY flows (from prey to predator). A food web is many interlinked food chains, giving a more realistic picture of an ecosystem.
As a rule of thumb, only about 10% of the energy at one trophic level is passed on to the next; roughly 90% is lost. Actual efficiencies vary (about 5–20%), so 10% is an approximation, not a law.

Mostly heat released by RESPIRATION at each level. Also: not all of an organism is eaten, and not all eaten material is digested/absorbed (energy leaves in faeces). These losses mean little energy reaches the next level.
A diagram of the energy (or rate of energy flow, kJ m⁻² yr⁻¹) at each trophic level. It is ALWAYS upright because energy is lost at every transfer, so each level holds less than the one below.
Because roughly 90% of energy is lost at each transfer, after only a few links too little energy remains to support another trophic level. This is why food chains rarely exceed four or five links.

Photosynthesis fixes CO₂ into organic carbon; respiration returns CO₂; feeding passes carbon along food chains; decomposition releases CO₂ from dead matter; combustion of biomass and fossil fuels releases CO₂ to the atmosphere.

flashcards.front: Role of decomposers in the carbon cycle
flashcards.back: They break down dead organisms and waste, respiring the organic carbon and returning CO₂ to the atmosphere (and mineral nutrients to the soil). Without them, carbon and nutrients would stay locked in dead matter.
NO. Matter cycles; energy does NOT. Energy flows one way — in as light, out as heat — and must be continually resupplied by the Sun. Saying 'energy is recycled' is a marked error.

flashcards.front: Exam trap: arrows in a food chain
flashcards.back: Arrows point in the direction ENERGY flows — from the organism being eaten TO the organism that eats it (prey → predator), NOT 'who eats whom'. Reversed arrows lose the mark.

quickCheck.prompt: Which statement correctly distinguishes energy and matter in an ecosystem?
options: Both energy and matter are recycled within the ecosystem
Energy flows through and is lost as heat, while matter is cycled and reused
Matter flows through and is lost, while energy is recycled
Neither energy nor matter enters or leaves an ecosystem
quickCheck.answer: Energy flows through and is lost as heat, while matter is cycled and reused

quickCheck.prompt: About 90% of the energy at one trophic level does not reach the next. The single largest reason is that:
options: It is reflected back to the Sun
It is lost as heat released during respiration
It is stored permanently in the soil
It is converted into new species
quickCheck.answer: It is lost as heat released during respiration

quickCheck.prompt: In an ecosystem, the group that breaks down dead organic matter and returns inorganic nutrients to the environment is the:
options: Producers
Primary consumers
Decomposers
Tertiary consumers

quickCheck.prompt: Which process in the carbon cycle REMOVES carbon dioxide from the atmosphere?
options: Respiration
Combustion
Photosynthesis
Decomposition

quickCheck.prompt: Food chains rarely have more than four or five links. The best explanation is that:
options: Predators cannot physically catch enough prey
So little energy remains after repeated ~90% losses that higher levels cannot be supported
There are not enough different species in most ecosystems
Energy is recycled and runs out
quickCheck.answer: So little energy remains after repeated ~90% losses that higher levels cannot be supported

faq.q: If energy and matter both move through a food chain, why do we say one flows and the other cycles?
faq.a: Follow a carbon atom and a joule of energy. The carbon atom can be eaten, respired back to CO₂, taken up again by a plant, eaten again — it circulates indefinitely between organisms and the environment, so matter CYCLES. The joule, once released as heat by respiration, radiates away and cannot be recaptured by the ecosystem; every level loses most of its energy this way. So energy FLOWS one direction — in as light, out as heat — and must be constantly resupplied by the Sun, while the finite pool of atoms is reused.

faq.q: Where exactly does the '90% lost' energy go between trophic levels?
faq.a: Three places. First and largest, RESPIRATION: organisms respire much of their food to power movement, growth and (in mammals and birds) staying warm, releasing that energy as heat. Second, not all of an organism is eaten — roots, bones, fur and wood are often left. Third, of what is eaten, not all is digested and absorbed; the indigestible part leaves in faeces. What remains — only about a tenth — is built into new biomass and is all that the next level can consume.

faq.q: Why is a pyramid of energy always upright when pyramids of numbers can be inverted?
faq.a: A pyramid of energy plots the energy (or rate of energy flow) at each level. Because energy is lost at every transfer, each level must contain less than the one below, so the pyramid can never be top-heavy. A pyramid of numbers just counts organisms, ignoring their size: a single large oak tree (one producer) can feed thousands of insects (many primary consumers), giving a narrow base and wide second tier — an inverted shape that says nothing false about energy, because that one tree still holds far more energy than all the insects.

faq.q: How do decomposers fit into the carbon cycle and energy flow?
faq.a: In the carbon cycle they are essential: by breaking down dead organisms and waste they respire the organic carbon back to CO₂ and free mineral nutrients into the soil, so producers can reuse them — without decomposers, carbon and nutrients would stay locked in dead matter. In energy terms they still obey the same rules: they take energy from dead material at every trophic level and lose most of it as heat through their own respiration, which is why energy never returns to the living food chain.

faq.q: How does human activity disturb these cycles?
faq.a: Chiefly by moving carbon faster than natural cycling can balance. Burning fossil fuels (combustion) releases carbon that had been locked away for millions of years, and deforestation both removes photosynthesising producers and, when the wood is burned or decomposed, adds more CO₂. The result is a rise in atmospheric CO₂ that outpaces uptake by photosynthesis and the oceans. Overharvesting top consumers and simplifying food webs also disturbs energy flow, but at C4.2 you only need the outline: human activity adds CO₂ to the atmosphere faster than the carbon cycle removes it.

sections.content: Why is a lion rarer than a gazelle, and a gazelle rarer than the grass it eats? Why do food chains almost never run beyond four or five links? The answers come from two contrasting rules that govern every ecosystem. Energy enters as sunlight, is captured by producers and handed along a food chain — but leaks away as heat at every step, so it flows one way and is gone. Matter behaves quite differently: the atoms organisms are built from are finite, so they are cycled endlessly, released by decomposers and taken up again by producers. This lesson works through both — trophic levels and feeding relationships, the ten-percent ceiling on energy transfer, the carbon cycle, and briefly how humans disturb it — and shows how to write the answers the way our marking engine awards the marks.
Ecosystems as open systems: energy flows, matter cycles

An ecosystem is an **open system**: it exchanges both energy and matter with its surroundings. But the two are exchanged in fundamentally different ways. **Energy flows through** an ecosystem in one direction — it enters (in most ecosystems) as light, is converted to chemical energy by producers, is passed between organisms, and is ultimately lost to the surroundings as heat. It is never recycled, so it must be continually resupplied by the Sun. **Matter cycles within** an ecosystem. The chemical elements that make up organisms — carbon, nitrogen, phosphorus and the rest — are finite. They pass from the environment into organisms and back again, circulating between the living (biotic) and non-living (abiotic) components, driven above all by decomposers. Holding these two ideas apart — flow versus cycle — is the single most important thing in this topic.

items: **Energy FLOWS:** one direction only — in as light, out as heat; lost at every step; never recycled; needs constant resupply from the Sun.
**Matter CYCLES:** the same finite atoms are used again and again, passing between organisms and the environment.
**Open system:** an ecosystem exchanges both energy and matter with its surroundings.
The commonest error in this whole topic is saying energy is 'recycled' — it is not.

sections.content: Trophic levels: producers, consumers and decomposers

A **trophic level** is an organism's feeding position — how many steps its energy is from the original source. **Producers** (autotrophs such as plants and algae) form the first level: they make their own organic compounds, usually by photosynthesis, and are the entry point of energy into the ecosystem. **Consumers** (heterotrophs) obtain energy by feeding on other organisms. A **primary consumer** (herbivore) eats producers; a **secondary consumer** eats primary consumers; a **tertiary consumer** eats secondary consumers. Standing apart from this ladder are the **decomposers** — chiefly saprotrophic bacteria and fungi — which feed on dead organic matter and waste from every level, breaking it down and releasing inorganic nutrients back to the environment. Decomposers are what make matter cycling possible.

items: **Producers:** autotrophs (photosynthesise); first trophic level; the energy entry point.
**Consumers:** heterotrophs — primary (eat producers), secondary (eat primary), tertiary (eat secondary).
**Decomposers:** feed on dead matter and waste from all levels; release inorganic nutrients — the key to nutrient cycling.
Producers make organic matter; decomposers break it down — do not confuse the two.

sections.content: Food chains and food webs

A **food chain** shows a single sequence of feeding relationships, for example: *grass → grasshopper → shrew → owl*. The arrows are critical: each arrow points in the direction that **energy flows**, from the organism being eaten to the organism that eats it (prey → predator). Real ecosystems are far more tangled than a single chain, because most organisms eat, and are eaten by, several species. A **food web** joins many food chains together to give a realistic map of who feeds on whom. Reading a web, you can trace how energy captured by producers is distributed through the whole community — and see why removing one species can ripple through many others.

Draw and read food-chain arrows as ENERGY arrows: they run from prey to predator (grass → rabbit → fox), never the other way. A surprising number of marks are lost simply by drawing the arrows backwards, as though they meant 'eats'.
Energy transfer and the 10% rule

The transfer of energy from one trophic level to the next is strikingly inefficient. As a rule of thumb, only about **10%** of the energy at one level is passed on to the next; roughly **90% is lost**. (The real figure varies, commonly between about 5% and 20%, so 10% is an approximation rather than a law.) The energy does not vanish — it goes to three places. Most is lost as **heat from respiration**: organisms respire much of their food to power movement, growth and, in birds and mammals, keeping warm. On top of that, **not all of an organism is eaten** (roots, bones, fur and wood are often left), and of what is eaten, **not all is digested and absorbed** — the indigestible remainder leaves in faeces. Only the small fraction built into new biomass is available to the next level.

items: **Respiration (largest loss):** energy used for life processes is released as heat and leaves the ecosystem.
**Not all eaten:** parts of organisms (roots, bones, fur, wood) are never consumed.
**Not all digested:** of the material eaten, the indigestible part is lost in faeces.
**Result:** only about 10% is stored as new biomass and passed on — hence the ~90% loss.

sections.content: Two consequences follow directly. First, **food chains are short**: after only a few ~90% losses, so little energy remains that no further trophic level can be supported — which is why chains rarely exceed four or five links. Second, a **pyramid of energy** (the energy, or rate of energy flow in kJ m⁻² yr⁻¹, at each level) is **always upright**, because each level must hold less than the one below. Pyramids of numbers or biomass can occasionally be inverted — a single tree feeding many insects, for instance — but a pyramid of energy never can.

sections.question: A meadow's producers store 40 000 kJ m⁻² yr⁻¹ of energy as new biomass. The primary consumers that feed on them store 3200 kJ m⁻² yr⁻¹. (a) Calculate the percentage efficiency of energy transfer from producers to primary consumers. (b) State whether this is consistent with the 10% rule, and explain in one sentence where most of the 'missing' energy has gone. [4]
sections.solution:
**(a) Percentage efficiency.**
Efficiency (%) = (energy stored at the higher level ÷ energy available at the lower level) × 100.  [M1: correct method]
Efficiency = (3200 ÷ 40 000) × 100 = 8.0%.  [A1: 8.0%]

**(b) Consistency and explanation.**
8.0% is close to the ~10% rule of thumb, which predicts roughly a tenth of the energy is passed on, so the value is consistent (the rule is only an approximation, typically 5–20%).  [A1: correct comparison]
Most of the missing ~92% was lost as heat from respiration by the producers and consumers, with smaller amounts never eaten or lost in faeces.  [A1: main loss identified as respiratory heat]

*Always divide the energy that reached the NEW level by the energy that was available at the PREVIOUS level — reversing the fraction is a common slip.*

sections.question: The producers in a pond fix 50 000 kJ m⁻² yr⁻¹ of energy. Assuming exactly 10% is transferred at each step, estimate the energy available to the tertiary consumers, and use your answer to explain why the pond cannot support a fifth trophic level. [4]
sections.solution:
**Step 1 — apply the 10% rule level by level.** Each arrow passes on one tenth of the energy.
- Producers: 50 000 kJ m⁻² yr⁻¹.
- Primary consumers: 10% of 50 000 = 5000 kJ m⁻² yr⁻¹.  [M1: 10% applied correctly]
- Secondary consumers: 10% of 5000 = 500 kJ m⁻² yr⁻¹.
- Tertiary consumers: 10% of 500 = 50 kJ m⁻² yr⁻¹.  [A1: 50 kJ m⁻² yr⁻¹]

**Step 2 — the next level.** A fourth-order (quaternary) consumer would receive only 10% of 50 = 5 kJ m⁻² yr⁻¹.  [M1: continues the pattern]

**Step 3 — explain.** After four transfers, so little energy remains (about 5 kJ m⁻² yr⁻¹, one ten-thousandth of the original) that it cannot meet the respiratory needs of another trophic level, so no fifth level can be sustained. This repeated ~90% loss is why food chains are short.  [A1: links tiny residual energy to limit on chain length]

*Notice each step is a factor of ten — the energy collapses fast, which is exactly why pyramids of energy are steep and always upright.*

sections.content: The recycling of matter: the carbon cycle

While energy drains away, the atoms are conserved and cycled — and the clearest example is the **carbon cycle**. Carbon dioxide is removed from the atmosphere by **photosynthesis**, which fixes it into the organic compounds of producers. That organic carbon then moves along food chains by **feeding**, as consumers eat other organisms. Carbon returns to the atmosphere as CO₂ through **respiration** by every organism, and through **decomposition**, in which decomposers break down dead organisms and waste and respire their carbon. Finally, **combustion** — the burning of biomass, and of fossil fuels formed from ancient organisms — releases CO₂ as well. Decomposers are pivotal: by releasing carbon and mineral nutrients from dead matter, they return the raw materials of life to the environment so producers can take them up again. This is why matter cycles indefinitely while energy does not.

items: **Photosynthesis:** removes CO₂ from the atmosphere, fixing carbon into producer biomass.
**Feeding:** transfers organic carbon along food chains from one organism to the next.
**Respiration:** returns CO₂ to the atmosphere from all living organisms.
**Decomposition:** decomposers break down dead matter and waste, respiring its carbon back to CO₂ and freeing nutrients.
**Combustion:** burning biomass and fossil fuels releases CO₂ to the atmosphere.

sections.content: Human impact on energy flow and the carbon cycle

Human activity disturbs these balanced flows — most importantly by moving carbon faster than the cycle can absorb. Burning **fossil fuels** releases carbon that had been locked away for millions of years, and **deforestation** both removes photosynthesising producers and, when the timber is burned or left to decompose, adds still more CO₂. Because this input outpaces removal by photosynthesis and the oceans, atmospheric CO₂ rises, driving climate change. Human pressure on food webs — overharvesting top consumers, simplifying communities — also disrupts energy flow through ecosystems. At this level you need only the outline: human activity adds CO₂ to the atmosphere faster than the carbon cycle can remove it, unbalancing a cycle that was previously in near-equilibrium.
Common mistakes examiners penalise

items: **Saying energy is 'recycled'** — energy FLOWS through and is lost as heat; only MATTER cycles. This is the single most penalised confusion in the topic.
**Drawing food-chain arrows backwards** — arrows point in the direction energy flows, from prey to predator, not 'who eats whom'.

**Explaining the 90% loss vaguely** — you must name the losses: heat from RESPIRATION (the main one), material not eaten, and material not digested (lost in faeces). 'Energy is used up' scores nothing.
**Confusing producers and decomposers** — producers MAKE organic matter (photosynthesis); decomposers BREAK it DOWN and release nutrients. They are opposite roles.
**Treating the 10% rule as an exact law** — it is an approximation (about 5–20%); state it as a rule of thumb, not a fixed figure.
**Saying a pyramid of energy 'can be inverted'** — pyramids of numbers and biomass can be, but a pyramid of energy is ALWAYS upright because energy is lost at every step.
**Muddling carbon-cycle processes** — photosynthesis REMOVES CO₂; respiration, decomposition and combustion RETURN it. Don't assign a process to the wrong direction.
**Forgetting decomposers in nutrient cycling** — nutrients cannot return to producers without decomposition; omitting decomposers leaves the cycle broken.

sections.content: Model answer — marked the way our engine marks it

This is an EXPLAIN question, and explain marks are awarded analytically — each distinct valid biological point is worth one mark, up to the number of marks available. There is no need for a fixed wording: the engine looks for separate creditable ideas, awards the answer mark (A) for a correctly reasoned conclusion, applies error-carried-forward (ECF) so an earlier slip does not sink later points, and accepts equivalent phrasings. Study how each mark below is tied to a specific, named idea.

sections.question: Explain why the amount of energy available decreases at each successive trophic level in a food chain. [4]
sections.solution:
**Model answer.**
At each trophic level, organisms lose much of their energy as heat through respiration, because they respire to power life processes such as movement and growth. In addition, not all of an organism at one level is eaten by the next, and of the material that is eaten, not all is digested and absorbed — the indigestible part is lost in faeces. As a result, only about 10% of the energy at one level is stored as new biomass and so passed on to the next. Because roughly 90% is lost at every transfer, the energy available falls sharply at each successive level, which is also why food chains are limited in length.

---
**How our marking engine awards the 4 marks** — each distinct valid point scores 1 (maximum 4):

- **Point 1 (M):** energy is lost as HEAT through RESPIRATION at each trophic level.
- **Point 2 (M):** not all of an organism is eaten by the next trophic level.
- **Point 3 (M):** not all of what is eaten is digested/absorbed — energy is lost in faeces.
- **Point 4 (M):** so only about 10% is passed on / ~90% is lost at each transfer.
- **Conclusion (A):** therefore energy available decreases at each successive level, limiting food-chain length. Awarded for a correctly reasoned conclusion, not a restatement.

Because five creditable points are shown for a 4-mark question, the engine caps the award at 4 — there is deliberate room to miss one point and still score full marks. But marks are for DISTINCT ideas: writing 'energy is lost' twice in different words scores once. **A** is given for the reasoned conclusion; **ECF** means an earlier wrong figure does not cost the later points; and equivalent wordings are accepted (e.g. 'lost through metabolism/heat' for respiration, 'egested/lost as faeces' for undigested material).

**What the engine rejects.** 'The energy is used up' or 'the animals need it to live' with no named process scores nothing — the mark scheme wants the specific losses (respiration/heat, not eaten, not digested). Saying energy is 'recycled' or that it is 'lost to the next animal that eats it' is contradicted and scores zero for that point.

**Bottom line:** name the three losses (respiration/heat, not all eaten, not all digested), state the ~10% transfer, and close with the reasoned consequence. Each valid line stands on its own.

sections.content: The Practice button marks YOUR answer this same way — point by point, with ECF and a cap at the marks available. So write in distinct, creditable statements: name respiration and heat, say not all is eaten, say not all is digested, give the ~10% figure, then state the consequence. Vague phrases like 'energy is used up' feel like an answer but earn nothing; the engine rewards the named biological processes, and showing each one is what protects your score.
Where this leads

The two rules of this topic — energy flows, matter cycles — underpin the rest of ecology. The ten-percent ceiling explains why eating lower on a food chain feeds more people from the same land, and why top predators are always few. Nutrient cycling links straight to the nitrogen and phosphorus cycles and to conservation, while the carbon cycle connects your biology to the physics of the greenhouse effect and to the whole question of human-driven climate change. Hold the core distinction firmly — energy in as light and out as heat, atoms round and round forever — and the rest of ecosystem ecology falls into place.
